proposal_text: This project proposes to install camera-based emotion recognition systems in six secondary school classrooms in Dublin to study the relationship between student emotional engagement and learning outcomes. The system will use computer vision to classify student facial expressions into six emotional categories (happy, sad, angry, surprised, fearful, neutral) at 5-second intervals throughout lessons. Data will be collected over one academic term covering approximately 180 students aged 13-17. Parental consent will be obtained but students will not be given an individual opt-out mechanism that allows them to remain in the classroom without being monitored. The emotion recognition system has been validated primarily on adult Western faces and has not been tested for accuracy on adolescent faces or faces from diverse ethnic backgrounds. Aggregated emotional engagement scores will be shared with teachers to inform pedagogical adjustments.

assessment.risk_summary: The proposed research project involves the installation of camera-based emotion recognition systems in secondary school classrooms, which raises concerns about student privacy, potential biases in facial expression classification, and the lack of explicit opt-out mechanisms for students. The project also involves processing personal data without explicit consent from vulnerable groups (adolescents).

identified_risks.risk: Processing special category data about vulnerable groups without explicit consent
identified_risks.explanation: The project involves collecting and processing biometric data from adolescents, a vulnerable group, without their explicit consent.

identified_risks.risk: Potential biases in facial expression classification
identified_risks.explanation: The emotion recognition system has been validated primarily on adult Western faces and may not be accurate for adolescent faces or diverse ethnic backgrounds.

identified_risks.risk: Lack of explicit opt-out mechanisms for students
identified_risks.explanation: Students will not have an individual opt-out mechanism to remain in the classroom without being monitored.

historical_precedents.incident_title: Detroit Police Facial Recognition False Arrest
historical_precedents.lesson: Facial recognition technology can lead to false arrests and discrimination against certain groups
The use of facial recognition technology in schools raises concerns about student privacy and potential biases in classification

recommended_mitigations.mitigation: Implement explicit opt-out mechanisms for students
Ensure that the emotion recognition system is validated on diverse datasets, including adolescent faces and faces from diverse ethnic backgrounds

tier1_mandatory_actions: Obtain explicit consent from students and parents
Comply with GDPR requirements for processing special category data
tier2_reflective_prompts: Consider potential harms and mitigation strategies
Discuss the implications of using facial recognition technology in schools
